issues.original: I have shipped four agent systems on Alibaba Cloud in the last eighteen months. Three of them started life as a [CODE] session on a single ECS instance someone created by clicking through the console. All three of those needed a panicked weekend of rebuilding when the second engineer joined the project, when the prod region had a stockout, or when the security team asked for a network diagram.
issues.fixed: In the last eighteen months, I've shipped four agent systems on Alibaba Cloud. Three began as a [CODE] session on a single ECS instance created via the console. Each required a frantic weekend of rebuilding when a second engineer joined, the prod region had a stockout, or the security team requested a network diagram.

issues.original: The fourth started life as [CODE]. It was the only one I haven't lost a weekend to.
issues.fixed: The fourth started as [CODE], and it's the only one that didn't cost me a weekend.

issues.original: This series is the field guide for that fourth pattern: how to use Terraform to provision the cloud infrastructure that an AI agent system actually needs on Alibaba Cloud. It is not a Terraform tutorial — there are good ones online and the official [CODE] doc covers the basics. It is the senior-engineer playbook for the specific intersection of 'I run agents' and 'I run them on Aliyun'.
issues.fixed: This series is a field guide for the fourth pattern: using Terraform to provision the cloud infrastructure an AI agent system needs on Alibaba Cloud. It's not a Terraform tutorial—there are plenty online, and the official [CODE] doc covers the basics. This is the senior-engineer playbook for running agents on Aliyun.

issues.original: Eight articles. One real, working stack at the end. This first one is the why.
issues.fixed: Eight articles. One real, working stack at the end. This first one explains the why.

issues.original: Before we talk infrastructure, let's name the components an agent system has — the ones a [CODE] README usually skips:
issues.fixed: Before discussing infrastructure, let's list the components an agent system has—the ones a [CODE] README often skips:

issues.original: That is at least nine separate Aliyun services touching each other in specific ways. Each has its own console page, its own RAM permissions, its own region scoping, its own networking. The probability that you can wire all of this up by hand and have it still match across [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE] after three months of evolution is roughly zero.
issues.fixed: That's at least nine separate Aliyun services, each with its own console page, RAM permissions, region scoping, and networking. The chance of manually wiring all of this and having it match across [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE] after three months is nearly zero.

issues.original: Nine services touched by hand is also nine drift surfaces. The pain pattern is universal enough that I have a stock figure for it:
issues.fixed: Nine services managed manually create nine drift surfaces. This pain pattern is so common that I have a standard figure for it:

issues.original: Read the left column carefully. Every step is plausible — none of them are dumb mistakes. They are what happens when smart people make small reasonable decisions over months. The right column is the same path, but every step leaves an artifact in git. The diff between the two columns is the difference between 'I shipped this' and 'I am paged at 2am because nobody knows what's running in [CODE].'"
issues.fixed: Read the left column carefully. Every step is plausible—none are dumb mistakes. They result from smart people making small, reasonable decisions over time. The right column shows the same path, but every step leaves an artifact in git. The difference between the two columns is the difference between 'I shipped this' and 'I'm paged at 2 AM because no one knows what's running in [CODE].'"

issues.original: Terraform is an open-source declarative tool from HashiCorp. You write [CODE] files in **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)** that describe the cloud resources you want; Terraform diffs that desired state against the live state recorded in a **state file** and emits a **plan**; you review the plan; you [CODE] it; Terraform translates the plan into provider API calls.
issues.fixed: Terraform is an open-source declarative tool from HashiCorp. You write [CODE] files in **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)** to describe the cloud resources you want. Terraform compares the desired state with the live state in a **state file** and generates a **plan**. You review the plan, apply it, and Terraform translates the plan into provider API calls.

issues.original: Three things to internalize from that:
issues.fixed: Three key points to remember:

issues.original: Every agent stack I've shipped has at some point been audited — by a security review, by finance reconciling cloud spend, by a new SRE trying to figure out what's actually running. In every case the question is the same: *what exists, who created it, and what is it costing me?*
issues.fixed: Every agent stack I've shipped has been audited at some point—by a security review, by finance reconciling cloud spend, or by a new SRE trying to figure out what's running. The question is always the same: *what exists, who created it, and what is it costing me?*

issues.original: If your infra lives in a Terraform state file, that question takes 30 seconds to answer:
issues.fixed: If your infrastructure is in a Terraform state file, that question takes 30 seconds to answer:

issues.original: For the four agent stacks I run today, those three commands produce a comprehensive inventory in seconds. Before Terraform, the same audit involved opening twelve console tabs across ECS, VPC, RDS, OSS, RAM, KMS, SLS, ARMS, ACK, CloudMonitor, ALB, and OpenSearch — each one filtered by tag if I was lucky and by gut feeling if I wasn't.
issues.fixed: For the four agent stacks I run today, those three commands produce a comprehensive inventory in seconds. Before Terraform, the same audit required opening twelve console tabs across ECS, VPC, RDS, OSS, RAM, KMS, SLS, ARMS, ACK, CloudMonitor, ALB, and OpenSearch—filtered by tags if I was lucky, and by gut feeling if I wasn't.

issues.original: This is the article where you stop reading and start typing. By the end you will have:

issues.original: Nothing here provisions an agent yet. We are laying the foundation that every later article assumes. If you skip this and try to wing it in article 3, you will eat a state-corruption incident inside a week.
issues.fixed: Nothing here provisions an agent yet. We are laying the foundation that every later article assumes. If you skip this and try to wing it in article 3, you'll likely face a state-corruption incident within a week.

issues.original: I won't dwell — the official [CODE] doc covers all OSes. On macOS:
issues.fixed: I won't dwell; the official [CODE] doc covers all OSes. On macOS:

issues.original: Pin to a recent stable. The Aliyun docs are tested against [CODE], but on a fresh project use [CODE]. There are real ergonomic improvements in newer versions — [CODE], [CODE], refined [CODE] blocks, the declarative [CODE] block — and you will use all of them in this series.
issues.fixed: Pin to a recent stable. The Aliyun docs are tested against [CODE], but for a fresh project, use [CODE]. Newer versions offer real ergonomic improvements — [CODE], [CODE], refined [CODE] blocks, and the declarative [CODE] block — which you will use throughout this series.

issues.original: The [CODE] constraint allows [CODE] through [CODE] but blocks [CODE]. This is the right default. Once you commit [CODE] to git (Terraform creates it on [CODE]), you also lock the *exact* provider version and its checksum. If a teammate runs [CODE] later, they get the same provider — bit-identical.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] constraint allows [CODE] through [CODE] but blocks [CODE]. This is the right default. Once you commit [CODE] to git (Terraform creates it on [CODE]), you lock the exact provider version and its checksum. If a teammate runs [CODE] later, they get the same provider — bit-identical.

issues.original: Pinning early is cheap insurance. The alicloud provider has shipped breaking changes between minor versions (the OSS bucket schema rework around 1.220 ate three of my afternoons). You will eventually need to upgrade — do it deliberately, in a PR, with the diff in plan output, not by accident on a teammate's laptop at 11pm.
issues.fixed: Pinning early is cheap insurance. The alicloud provider has introduced breaking changes between minor versions (the OSS bucket schema rework around 1.220 cost me three afternoons). You will eventually need to upgrade — do it deliberately in a PR, with the diff in plan output, not by accident on a teammate's laptop at 11pm.

issues.original: The provider needs Aliyun credentials. There are three real choices, in increasing order of professional acceptability:
issues.fixed: The provider needs Aliyun credentials. Here are three options, ranked by professional acceptability:

issues.original: If the AK/SK is for a RAM sub-account scoped to only the resources Terraform manages, this is acceptable for a solo project. For anything shared, skip to option B.
issues.fixed: If the AK/SK is for a RAM sub-account scoped to only the resources Terraform manages, it's acceptable for a solo project. For anything shared, skip to option B.

issues.original: CI runners shouldn't carry long-lived AKs. Give the runner an AK with one permission only — [CODE] on a target role — and have Terraform assume that role at apply time:
issues.fixed: CI runners shouldn't carry long-lived AKs. Give the runner an AK with one permission only — [CODE] on a target role — and have Terraform assume that role at apply time.

issues.original: Zero secrets in any config, in any env var, in any file. Rotation is automatic. This is the gold standard, and it is the path I push every team toward within their first month.
issues.fixed: Zero secrets in any config, env var, or file. Rotation is automatic. This is the gold standard, and it's the path I push every team toward within their first month.

issues.original: When you run [CODE], by default Terraform writes [CODE] in the current directory. That file is the source of truth for what infrastructure exists. Three things will go wrong:
issues.fixed: When you run [CODE], Terraform writes [CODE] in the current directory by default. This file is the source of truth for your infrastructure. Three things can go wrong:

issues.original: A common misconception: tfstate just stores resource IDs. False. The state file contains every attribute Terraform knows about every resource — including computed ones like RDS connection strings, generated passwords, KMS key material, and [CODE] variables.
issues.fixed: A common misconception: tfstate only stores resource IDs. False. The state file contains every attribute Terraform knows about each resource, including computed ones like RDS connection strings, generated passwords, KMS key material, and [CODE] variables.

issues.original: OSS holds the actual [CODE] file (with versioning enabled — recovery is one CLI command if something corrupts). Tablestore holds a tiny "lock" row that Terraform writes before any apply and deletes after. If a second [CODE] starts while the first holds the lock, the second one waits or fails — never both running at once.
issues.fixed: OSS holds the actual [CODE] file (with versioning enabled — recovery is one CLI command if something corrupts). Tablestore holds a tiny "lock" row that Terraform writes before any apply and deletes afterward. If a second [CODE] starts while the first holds the lock, the second one waits or fails — never both running at once.

issues.original: The value still lives in tfstate (encrypted), but at least your GitHub Actions log doesn't paste the password into the world.
issues.fixed: The value still lives in tfstate (encrypted), but at least your GitHub Actions log won't expose the password.

issues.original: This article builds the single most copied piece of Terraform in my agent projects: a [CODE] module that gives every later component (ECS, RDS, OpenSearch, ACK) a sane place to land. It's about 200 lines of HCL all-in. Worth typing once, refer to it forever.
issues.fixed: This article builds the most frequently copied piece of Terraform in my agent projects: a [CODE] module that provides a sensible landing place for each subsequent component (ECS, RDS, OpenSearch, ACK). It's about 200 lines of HCL. Type it once and refer to it forever.

issues.original: Before code, the picture: Why three zones? Because Aliyun reserves the right to do a zone-level maintenance on any given Sunday, and a single-zone deployment means your agents are offline for the whole window. Cross-zone traffic inside a VPC is free; the only cost of three zones is the operational complexity of subnet math, which Terraform absorbs for you.
issues.fixed: Before we dive into the code, here's why three zones: Aliyun can perform zone-level maintenance on any given Sunday, and a single-zone deployment would mean your agents are offline during the entire window. Cross-zone traffic within a VPC is free; the only cost of using three zones is the operational complexity of subnet math, which Terraform handles for you.

issues.original: Why public + private? The agent runtime should live in private vSwitches so a misconfigured security group can't accidentally expose it on [CODE]. Public vSwitches hold the ALB and the NAT Gateway — things that *must* reach the internet. The agent reaches the internet via NAT, never directly.
issues.fixed: Why public and private vSwitches? The agent runtime should be in private vSwitches to prevent accidental exposure due to a misconfigured security group. Public vSwitches host the ALB and NAT Gateway, which must reach the internet. The agent accesses the internet via NAT, not directly.

issues.original: Forcing exactly three zones is opinionated but matches the diagram. If you need two-zone or four-zone, fork the module — don't make it conditional. Conditional modules become unreadable, and unreadable modules get rewritten from scratch six months later.
issues.fixed: Requiring exactly three zones is opinionated but aligns with the diagram. If you need two or four zones, fork the module—don't make it conditional. Conditional modules become unreadable and often get rewritten from scratch six months later.

issues.original: The SNAT entries are what actually let private-subnet instances reach the internet. Without them, an agent in [CODE] cannot resolve [CODE] — and you will spend an hour debugging that the first time it bites you. I have the scar.
issues.fixed: SNAT entries allow private-subnet instances to reach the internet. Without them, an agent in [CODE] can't resolve [CODE], and you'll spend an hour debugging the issue the first time it happens. I've been there.

issues.original: By the time you're done, attaching an ECS to the right SG is just [CODE] and the network tier is correct by construction. Audit becomes trivial: you grep for the SG name and find every resource that ever held it.
issues.fixed: By the end, attaching an ECS to the right SG is as simple as [CODE], and the network tier is correct by construction. Auditing is straightforward: grep for the SG name to find every resource that used it.

issues.original: Why the alias? Because the CMK ID is a UUID nobody remembers; the alias [CODE] is human-readable and stable across key rotations. Reference the alias from RDS, OSS, and SLS, and you can swap the underlying key without touching downstream config.
issues.fixed: Why use an alias? The CMK ID is a UUID that no one remembers, while the alias [CODE] is human-readable and remains stable across key rotations. Referencing the alias from RDS, OSS, and SLS allows you to swap the underlying key without changing downstream configurations.

issues.original: Networks drift. Someone opens a port in the console at 11pm to debug a thing. Someone adds an SNAT rule to test a workaround. The route table gets a temporary entry that nobody removes. Six months later the prod VPC and the HCL diverge silently — until the next [CODE] either reverts the manual change (breaking whoever depended on it) or, worse, confuses the provider's update logic into recreating a resource.
issues.fixed: Networks can drift. Someone might open a port in the console at 11 PM to debug an issue. Another person might add an SNAT rule to test a workaround. A temporary entry in the route table might go unremoved. Six months later, the production VPC and the HCL silently diverge—until the next [CODE] either reverts the manual change (breaking whatever depends on it) or, worse, confuses the provider's update logic, leading to resource recreation.

issues.original: The single most important architecture decision in an agent system is *where the agent loop process actually runs*. There are three good answers on Aliyun, plus a fourth that almost everyone forgets. Picking the wrong one isn't catastrophic — you can migrate later — but it costs you weeks of unnecessary scaffolding and several thousand RMB a month in idle compute.
issues.fixed: The single most important architectural decision in an agent system is *where the agent loop process runs*. There are three good options on Aliyun, plus a fourth that almost everyone forgets. Picking the wrong one isn't catastrophic — you can migrate later — but it costs weeks of unnecessary work and several thousand RMB a month in idle compute.

issues.original: This article walks through all four with working Terraform, the cost crossover, and the operational gotchas I keep tripping on.
issues.fixed: This article covers all four options with working Terraform, cost crossovers, and operational gotchas I often encounter.

issues.original: ECS (Container Service for Kubernetes) is the prod answer at scale. Multiple agent kinds, autoscaling, rolling deploys, GPU scheduling. Worth the operational weight only when you have at least three or four agent services and an SRE who's comfortable in K8s.
issues.fixed: ACK (Container Service for Kubernetes) is the production solution at scale. It supports multiple agent types, autoscaling, rolling deployments, and GPU scheduling. It's worth the operational overhead if you have at least three or four agent services and an SRE comfortable with K8s.

issues.original: Function Compute (FC) is per-invocation, scale-to-zero. Cold start 200-800ms, hard cap of 24 hours per invocation. Right for webhook-triggered agents, scheduled crawlers, and anything that runs in bursts and idles otherwise.
issues.fixed: Function Compute (FC) scales to zero and is per-invocation. It has a cold start of 200-800ms and a hard cap of 24 hours per invocation. It's suitable for webhook-triggered agents, scheduled crawlers, and tasks that run in bursts and idle otherwise.

issues.original: Elastic Container Instance (ECI) is the one people forget — a container without a node underneath. Cold start ~5s, billed per second, no node pool to manage. The sweet spot is bursty batch jobs that run 2-30 minutes per shot, several times an hour.
issues.fixed: Elastic Container Instance (ECI) is often overlooked — it's a container without an underlying node. It has a cold start of about 5 seconds, is billed per second, and requires no node pool management. It's ideal for bursty batch jobs that run 2-30 minutes each, several times an hour.

issues.original: The first three cover most cases. ECI fills the gap between 'too long for FC' and 'too bursty for ECS'.
issues.fixed: The first three cover most cases. ECI fits the gap between 'too long for FC' and 'too bursty for ECS'.

issues.original: Here's the rough monthly cost picture as a function of sustained QPS: Under ~1 QPS sustained, FC dominates — you pay almost nothing during idle. From ~1 to ~30 QPS sustained, a single ECS box wins. Above that, ACK's higher fixed cost gets amortised over enough load to be cheaper than packing more onto ECS. ECI sits orthogonally: cheaper than ECS below 50% utilisation, more expensive above.
issues.fixed: Here's the rough monthly cost picture based on sustained QPS: Below ~1 QPS, FC is the best choice — you pay almost nothing during idle. Between ~1 and ~30 QPS, a single ECS instance is the winner. Above that, ACK's higher fixed cost becomes cheaper than adding more ECS instances. ECI is cheaper than ECS below 50% utilization but more expensive above that.

issues.original: The model is rough — your actual numbers depend on instance family, network, and how chatty the agent is — but the *shape* is reliable. The decision rule I use:
issues.fixed: The model is rough — your actual numbers depend on the instance family, network, and the agent's chattiness — but the *shape* is reliable. Here's the decision rule I use:

issues.original: An agent's memory is the part most tutorials hand-wave. "Just put the embeddings in Pinecone, the sessions in Postgres, the screenshots in S3." On Aliyun all three exist as managed services, and Terraform-provisioning them right is the difference between "memory works" and "we lost three weeks of conversation history because the disk filled up at 4am".
issues.fixed: An agent's memory is the part most tutorials gloss over. "Just put the embeddings in Pinecone, the sessions in Postgres, and the screenshots in S3." On Aliyun, all three are managed services, and provisioning them correctly with Terraform can mean the difference between a working memory and losing three weeks of conversation history because the disk filled up at 4 AM.

issues.original: This article covers all three layers, the Terraform for each, the boring-but-critical backup and DR plumbing, the major-version upgrade story, and the one Saturday outage that taught me everything I now do differently.
issues.fixed: This article covers all three layers, their Terraform configurations, the critical but tedious backup and disaster recovery (DR) setup, the major version upgrade process, and the Saturday outage that taught me everything I now do differently.

issues.original: Don't conflate them. I once watched a team try to put 50 GB of generated PDFs in Postgres because "it has a [CODE] column". Cost ten times what OSS would have, query latency went to mush, backups took hours. Each layer has a service that's good at exactly its job — pick the right one and the bill stays sane.
issues.fixed: Don't mix them up. I once saw a team try to store 50 GB of generated PDFs in Postgres because "it has a [CODE] column". This cost ten times more than OSS, increased query latency, and made backups take hours. Each layer has a service that excels at its specific task—choose the right one to keep costs under control.

issues.original: For session state — turn-by-turn conversation, tool-call traces, user identity — you want a real RDBMS. PostgreSQL is my default; MySQL works fine if your team prefers it. PolarDB is the next step up when you need horizontal scale.
issues.fixed: For session state—turn-by-turn conversation, tool call traces, and user identity—you need a robust RDBMS. PostgreSQL is my go-to, but MySQL is fine if your team prefers it. PolarDB is the next step up when you need horizontal scaling.

issues.original: What earns its line in this block: - **Password lives in KMS Secrets Manager from birth.** Generated by [CODE], written to [CODE], retrieved by the agent at startup via STS. Plaintext never leaves Terraform's memory and is referenced downstream by [CODE], not value, so it doesn't sit in tfstate.
issues.fixed: Key points: - **Password stored in KMS Secrets Manager from the start.** Generated by [CODE], written to [CODE], and retrieved by the agent at startup via STS. The plaintext never leaves Terraform's memory and is referenced downstream by [CODE], not by value, so it doesn't end up in the tfstate.

issues.original: A small relational layer is the spine. The next layer is what makes the agent feel smart.
issues.fixed: A small relational layer is the backbone. The next layer is what makes the agent feel intelligent.

issues.original: The app group is the OpenSearch concept that holds an index. From here you create the index schema via the OpenSearch console or SDK — the [CODE] resource exists but the schema bit is operational, not provisional. Pin embedding dimension (1536 for [CODE], 1024 for Aliyun's bge-m3) in the index settings and never change it; reindexing 10M vectors is a multi-day job.
issues.fixed: The app group in OpenSearch holds an index. You create the index schema via the OpenSearch console or SDK. The [CODE] resource exists, but the schema is operational, not provisional. Set the embedding dimension (1536 for [CODE], 1024 for Aliyun's bge-m3) in the index settings and never change it; reindexing 10M vectors takes multiple days.

issues.original: OSS is where artifacts go: generated images, PDFs, screenshots, run-trace tarballs, model checkpoints if you fine-tune. For an agent stack: Three things worth a closer look.
issues.fixed: OSS is where artifacts like generated images, PDFs, screenshots, run-trace tarballs, and model checkpoints (if you fine-tune) go. For an agent stack, three things are worth a closer look.

issues.original: For agent artifacts the rule says: keep 30 days hot, IA for two months, Archive for nine, Cold Archive for a year, then delete. For a 1 TB artifact corpus that's the difference between ~¥1500/mo (all Standard) and ~¥250/mo. Codify it in HCL once, save five figures over a year. The IA minimum-storage charge (30 days) and the Archive retrieval latency are the only footguns — don't put hot data in cold tiers and you're fine.
issues.fixed: For agent artifacts, the rule is: keep 30 days in Standard, two months in Infrequent Access, nine months in Archive, and one year in Cold Archive, then delete. For a 1 TB artifact corpus, this means the difference between ~¥1500/mo (all Standard) and ~¥250/mo. Codify this in HCL once to save significantly over a year. The only pitfalls are the 30-day minimum storage charge for IA and the Archive retrieval latency—avoid putting hot data in cold tiers.

issues.original: A pattern I see repeatedly in immature agent stacks: each agent has its own copy of [CODE] in its own [CODE] file. Sometimes the same key, sometimes different ones, sometimes a colleague's personal key from when they prototyped. When the bill arrives nobody can tell which agent caused which token spend, and when a key leaks (it always does) you're playing whack-a-mole across a dozen [CODE] files.
issues.fixed: In immature agent stacks, each agent often has its own copy of [CODE] in its own [CODE] file. The keys can be the same, different, or even a colleague's personal key from prototyping. When the bill comes, no one can trace which agent used which tokens. And when a key leaks (which it always does), you end up chasing down leaks across a dozen [CODE] files.

issues.original: The real wake-up call hit me two years ago. A contractor finished his three-month engagement on a Friday, his laptop went home, and on the following Tuesday DashScope billing flagged 12 million tokens of [CODE] traffic from an IP we didn't recognise. His personal API key — copy-pasted into a side project — was still sitting in our agent's [CODE]. Rotating it took six hours: three engineers, four repos, two CI pipelines, one panicked Slack thread. Never again.
issues.fixed: The real wake-up call came two years ago. A contractor finished his three-month engagement on a Friday, and by the following Tuesday, DashScope billing flagged 12 million tokens of [CODE] traffic from an unrecognized IP. His personal API key, copy-pasted into a side project, was still in our agent's [CODE]. It took six hours to rotate it: three engineers, four repos, two CI pipelines, and one panicked Slack thread. Never again.

issues.original: This article ends that pattern. We build one **LLM gateway** that:
issues.fixed: This article addresses that pattern. We build one **LLM gateway** that:

issues.original: You have two reasonable implementation options:
issues.fixed: You have two reasonable options:

issues.original: I use both depending on routing complexity. For a pure proxy with quotas, API Gateway alone is enough. For multi-provider routing with budget guards and circuit breakers — which is where every team ends up within six months — LiteLLM on ECS wins. The rest of this article goes with LiteLLM because that's what 80% of teams need.
issues.fixed: I use both, depending on routing complexity. For a simple proxy with quotas, API Gateway is enough. For multi-provider routing with budget guards and circuit breakers — which most teams need within six months — LiteLLM on ECS is better. The rest of this article focuses on LiteLLM because it suits 80% of teams.

issues.original: The first rule: provider keys never appear in [CODE] files, in [CODE] blocks, in agent code, or in tfstate plaintext. They live in KMS Secrets Manager and the gateway pulls them at startup via STS.
issues.fixed: The first rule: provider keys should never appear in [CODE] files, [CODE] blocks, agent code, or tfstate plaintext. They live in KMS Secrets Manager, and the gateway fetches them at startup via STS.

issues.original: Cost note: KMS Secrets Manager bills around ¥0.4 per secret per month plus ¥0.03 per 10k API calls in Shanghai region. For four provider keys pulled twice an hour by two gateway boxes, the monthly bill is rounding error — under ¥10. The default service KMS key is free; only customer-managed CMKs cost ¥1/month each. Don't let 'KMS sounds expensive' be the reason you stay on [CODE] files.
issues.fixed: Cost note: KMS Secrets Manager charges about ¥0.4 per secret per month and ¥0.03 per 10k API calls in the Shanghai region. For four provider keys pulled twice an hour by two gateway boxes, the monthly bill is negligible — under ¥10. The default service KMS key is free; only customer-managed CMKs cost ¥1/month each. Don't avoid KMS because it seems expensive.

issues.original: Three things deliberate here:
issues.fixed: Three key points here:

issues.original: LiteLLM is the easiest open-source LLM proxy I know of. It speaks the OpenAI API format on its frontend and translates to whatever each provider speaks on its backend. Self-hosting it on ECS keeps things flexible:
issues.fixed: LiteLLM is the easiest open-source LLM proxy I know. It uses the OpenAI API format on the frontend and translates to each provider's format on the backend. Self-hosting it on ECS keeps things flexible:

issues.original: Agents now reach the gateway at [CODE] and never see a provider key. Internal-only ALB — no public IP, no listener on 443 facing the world. If an agent needs to call from outside the VPC, it goes through the bastion or CEN, never directly.
issues.fixed: Agents now reach the gateway at [CODE] and never see a provider key. The ALB is internal-only, with no public IP or listener on port 443. If an agent needs to call from outside the VPC, it goes through the bastion or CEN, not directly.

issues.original: Agents are non-deterministic, multi-step, and call expensive APIs. The combination means you cannot debug them after the fact unless you instrumented them on day one. This article wires three pipelines through Terraform — logs, traces, metrics — into a unified dashboard, layers six SLS queries that solve real incidents, and provisions four alarms that have actually fired and saved my projects in production.
issues.fixed: Agents are non-deterministic, multi-step, and call expensive APIs. This combination means you can't debug them after the fact unless you instrumented them from the start. This article sets up three pipelines through Terraform — logs, traces, and metrics — into a unified dashboard, adds six SLS queries to solve real incidents, and sets up four alarms that have actually fired and saved my projects in production.

issues.original: By the end you have one DingTalk channel that pings before the bill explodes, the latency dies, the error rate spikes, or some agent starts looping on itself — plus the SLO budgets that turn ops feel into ops data.
issues.fixed: By the end, you'll have a DingTalk channel that alerts you before the bill explodes, latency increases, the error rate spikes, or an agent starts looping on itself — plus SLO budgets that turn operational feelings into data.

issues.original: The cheapest mistake here is shipping with only [CODE] to stdout. The most expensive is shipping with all three but never opening the dashboard until the first incident — at which point you discover your [CODE] field is sometimes [CODE] because the SDK didn't propagate context. Wire it on day one, then poke it weekly so you know it works.
issues.fixed: The cheapest mistake is shipping with only [CODE] to stdout. The most expensive is shipping with all three but never checking the dashboard until the first incident, when you find out your [CODE] field is sometimes [CODE] because the SDK didn't propagate context. Set it up on day one and check it weekly to ensure it works.

issues.original: The [CODE] store has a year retention because it's small and you need it years later when 'who changed the prod ALB on March 12' comes up. The 90-day window on [CODE] is the one I tune most often — short enough to keep storage under ¥30/mo at 5 GB/day, long enough to do quarter-over-quarter cost trending without a Hive job.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] store has a year retention because it's small and you'll need it years later for questions like 'who changed the prod ALB on March 12.' The 90-day window on [CODE] is the one I adjust most often — short enough to keep storage under ¥30/mo at 5 GB/day, and long enough for quarter-over-quarter cost trending without a Hive job.

issues.original: The reward: in ARMS you can see 'this agent run took 12s; 9s of it was the third LLM call to qwen-max.' That's the kind of visibility that actually changes how you build agents. The first time I saw a span tree from a real production session I rewrote the planner to skip retrieval when the user message was short — saved ~30% on p95 the same week.

The first time I saw a span tree from a real production session, I rewrote the planner to skip retrieval for short user messages, saving ~30% on p95 that week.

issues.original: This is where everything from articles 2 through 7 lands in one place. By the end you'll have run [CODE] once and produced a complete, observable, budgeted agent runtime stack on Alibaba Cloud — about 31 resources, ~7 minutes of wall clock, ¥12,530/month all-in at prod sizing.

issues.original: The stack we're building: Five layers — edge, compute, memory, platform, ops — composed from the modules we built across this series. Eleven Aliyun products under the hood: VPC, ECS, ALB, OSS, RDS for PostgreSQL, OpenSearch, KMS, SLS, ARMS, CloudMonitor, and DashScope (the LLM provider, accessed via the gateway).
issues.fixed: The stack we're building: five layers — edge, compute, memory, platform, and ops — composed from the modules we built in this series. Eleven Aliyun products are used: VPC, ECS, ALB, OSS, RDS for PostgreSQL, OpenSearch, KMS, SLS, ARMS, CloudMonitor, and DashScope (the LLM provider, accessed via the gateway).

issues.original: Eight [CODE] files at the top, five modules in [CODE], environment values in [CODE], secrets out of git in [CODE]. This is the layout I use on every project — boring is good. The thing I will not negotiate on is the [CODE] directory being in [CODE] from commit zero. Every leaked-key incident I've cleaned up traced back to someone adding the gitignore entry on commit 50 instead of commit 1.
issues.fixed: Eight [CODE] files at the top, five modules in [CODE], environment values in [CODE], and secrets out of git in [CODE]. This is the layout I use for every project — boring is good. Every leaked-key incident I've cleaned up traced back to someone adding the gitignore entry on commit 50 instead of commit 1.

issues.original: Five module calls. Each module takes the *previous* module's output as input — [CODE] reads [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. That dependency wiring is what Terraform uses to build the apply DAG:
issues.fixed: Five module calls. Each module takes the previous module's output as input — [CODE] reads [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. This dependency wiring is what Terraform uses to build the apply DAG:

issues.original: VPC and KMS sit at the top — they have no dependencies. Storage and gateway depend on VPC + KMS but are independent of each other, so Terraform builds them in parallel. Compute depends on all three because the cloud-init template needs their endpoints. Observability resources fan out at the end because they reference SG IDs from compute.
issues.fixed: VPC and KMS sit at the top with no dependencies. Storage and gateway depend on VPC and KMS but are independent of each other, so Terraform builds them in parallel. Compute depends on all three because the cloud-init template needs their endpoints. Observability resources fan out at the end, referencing SG IDs from compute.

issues.original: The [CODE] ternaries are the entire promotion strategy in three lines: prod gets HA RDS, two gateway instances, three agent ECS, ¥800 cost ceiling, cross-region DR. Dev gets the smallest viable shape. Same modules, different sizing, no environment-specific code paths to maintain.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] ternaries define the entire promotion strategy in three lines: prod gets HA RDS, two gateway instances, three agent ECS, a ¥800 cost ceiling, and cross-region DR. Dev gets the smallest viable shape. Same modules, different sizing, no environment-specific code paths to maintain.

issues.original: Real timing on a fresh apply: The wall-clock breakdown: - **0–60s:** VPC, vSwitch, NAT, EIP, KMS keys — fast resources - **60–380s:** RDS (5 minutes), OpenSearch (5.5 minutes), ECS (~2 minutes), gateway (~1.5 minutes) — all in parallel, gated by the slowest - **380–460s:** agent app deploy via cloud-init, observability resources, alarms
issues.fixed: Real timing on a fresh apply: - **0–60s:** VPC, vSwitch, NAT, EIP, KMS keys (fast resources) - **60–380s:** RDS (5 minutes), OpenSearch (5.5 minutes), ECS (~2 minutes), gateway (~1.5 minutes) — all in parallel, gated by the slowest - **380–460s:** agent app deployment via cloud-init, observability resources, and alarms

issues.original: About 7 minutes total, dominated by RDS and OpenSearch provisioning. Re-applies on no-change runs settle in under 30 seconds because Terraform only diffs.
issues.fixed: About 7 minutes total, dominated by RDS and OpenSearch provisioning. Re-applies on no-change runs take under 30 seconds because Terraform only diffs.

issues.original: That's a complete agent stack. ALB endpoint, gateway URL, the SLS dashboard URL — paste any of them into a browser and they work. The [CODE] output is computed in [CODE] from the workspace conditionals, so the number you see in plan matches what shows up on the bill (within ~10%, my long-running rule of thumb).
issues.fixed: That's a complete agent stack. ALB endpoint, gateway URL, and SLS dashboard URL — paste any of them into a browser, and they work. The [CODE] output is computed in [CODE] from the workspace conditionals, so the number you see in the plan matches what shows up on the bill (within ~10%, my long-running rule of thumb).
